gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Cough, nasal congestion, and rhinorrhea.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Randy Gutierrez is a 9-month-old male who is being evaluated today for a cough, nasal congestion, and rhinorrhea. He is accompanied by his mother.

Symptoms began yesterday around dinnertime with clear nasal drainage, pulling on his ears bilaterally, and rubbing his nose frequently. He does not have a barky cough or a sore throat. His rectal temperature was 100.3 degrees. The patient did consume a 7 ounce bottle this morning, but mostly denied the next offered bottle and declined cereal. He is irritable and having trouble falling asleep, described "like he is fighting it".

The patient has been exposed to an older sister who is sick and a cousin who has a sinus infection.

Treatment has included saline nasal spray, Tylenol, baby Vick's, and a humidifier. When the patient aroused this morning, he was warm to the touch. When his sleeper was removed, he was quite sweaty despite the air conditioner being on.

The patient's dandruff is also worsening. The only tried treatment is Aveeno shampoo.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Older sister.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

Tylenol PRN.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Temperature 100.3 degrees.

Ears, Eyes, Nose, Throat
Nasal congestion, clear mucus, rhinorrhea. Bilateral pulling of ears, no sore throat or hoarseness.

Respiratory
Positive for cough.

ASSESSMENT

 Viral infection
 Dandruff

PLAN

Viral infection
The patient presents with a viral infection and does not need antibiotics currently. I advised continuation of fluids, rest. Additionally, I suggested trying Zarbee's for children under 1 years old. If a croup cough develops, the patient will receive a 0.5 teaspoon.

I also provided counseling to the mother to avoid giving honey to the patient for his first year of life due to botulism spores.

Dandruff
The patient is experiencing worsening symptoms of dandruff. I recommended Head and Shoulders shampoo, Selsun Blue, or Nizoral A-D 2 times per week, combined with his usual shampoo. I counseled the mother that she should be careful not to get the shampoo in the patient eyes and to not use the extra strength shampoos. Additionally, he may also use baby oil and a soft brush on the area.

INSTRUCTIONS

Return in one week for a re-check.
notes:
Subjective:
The patient, a 2-year-old male, has been experiencing symptoms of a runny nose, stuffiness, cough, and pulling on his ears since last night. He also has been rubbing his nose excessively. The patient has a low-grade fever of 100.3°F, and his appetite has been decreased, eating only 7 ounces of formula from a bottle this morning and refusing to eat cereal. The patient's energy level is described as irritable, and he is having difficulty sleeping. The patient's older sister has been sick, and the patient's family has been exposed to a nephew with a sinus infection.

Current medications:
I prescribed acetaminophen (Tylenol) for fever reduction.

Past medical history:
The patient has a history of cradle cap.

The patient's ears looked okay during the examination.

Test Results:
[NOT MENTIONED]

Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has a viral upper respiratory infection; viral upper respiratory infection: active; cradle cap: active.

Plan:
I advised the patient's family to continue with fluids and rest. I recommended watching for a barky cough, which would necessitate giving the patient a half teaspoon of his sister's medicine. I also suggested using Zarbee's for the stuffy nose and a dose of Zarbee's for children under a year of age. The family was instructed to use the regular strength of Head and Shoulders, Selsun Blue, or Nizoral A.D. shampoo for the cradle cap, and to continue using the soft brush and baby oil to remove scales.

Follow-up:
The patient will be seen again in one week.

Subjective:
Randy Gutierrez, a 1-year-old male, presents with a 1-day history of symptoms. He started to feel unwell around supper time last night, with the onset of a runny nose, stuffiness, and cough. The patient's mucus is currently clear. He has been pulling on his ears and rubbing his nose. His rectal temperature was 100.3°F. He ate his dinner last night but had a poor appetite this morning, consuming only 7 ounces of formula from a bottle. He then got another bottle but barely ate from it. He was offered cereal but refused it. He appears irritable and has difficulty sleeping, fighting it hard to go to sleep. His energy level is variable. His older sister has been sick, and he was exposed to her. He also had a date with his nephew who had a sinus infection.

Plan:
I advised the patient's caregiver to continue with fluids, rest, and to watch for a barking cough. I prescribed Zarbee's for the stuffy nose, and in case of a barking cough, I instructed the caregiver to give the patient a half a teaspoon of his sister's medicine. I also recommended using Zarbee's for the regular cough, following the instructions for the dose for kids under a year of age. I advised against giving honey to the patient due to the risk of botulism spores. I recommended using a shampoo containing salicylic acid (such as Head and Shoulders, Selsun Blue, or Nizoral A.D.) to treat the cradle cap, using it twice a week, and avoiding the eyes when rinsing the hair.

The patient will be seen again in 1 week.
